ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a ham, scrambled egg and cheese croissant sandwich. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is used as the closest parent class. A more specific croissant sandwich term is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Sandwiches and filled rolls) is the closest defensible category. No specific croissant-based sandwich code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite sandwich product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 180 g, representing a standard-sized croissant sandwich as commonly sold at Singapore food courts, cafes, and school canteens.

nutrient_source.title: Ham, scrambled egg and cheese croissant — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Ham, scrambled egg and cheese croissant' (CR ID: F08020040). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 11.5 g/100g based on the fat profile of butter-laminated croissant dough combined with reduced-fat cheese and ham, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference values for analogous croissant sandwich components; this field is documented as an adapted estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are set to -1 as HPB does not report these values for this entry.

gi_source.title: Croissant, plain — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to croissant sandwich composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 67 for plain croissant (white flour, butter-laminated). This value is applied as the GI anchor for the croissant sandwich composite, as the croissant pastry dominates the carbohydrate fraction (20.98 g/100g). The protein and fat from ham, egg, and cheese will modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, but the GI is conservatively retained at 67 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 37.76) / 100 ≈ 25.3, rounded to 25.1 using 180 g serving × 20.98 g carbohydrate/100g = 37.76 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (445 mg/100g) with very low potassium (22 mg/100g), yielding an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that promotes fluid retention and elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; a standard 180 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium — more than one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit — and provides negligible potassium to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined croissant pastry provides high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~67, 21 g/100g) with very low dietary fibre (0.71 g/100g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation; high saturated fat may also worsen insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food; if consumed, portion should be restricted to half a croissant sandwich and paired with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (24.92 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat of ~11.5 g/100g from butter-laminated pastry and cheese, combined with elevated sodium (445 mg/100g) and cholesterol (14 mg/100g), presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption; the combination of high saturated fat, sodium, and refined carbohydrate contributes to dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated blood pressure, all of which are independent CVD risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g from butter and cheese) and processed ham (sodium nitrates, arachidonic acid) are pro-inflammatory, activating TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid croissant-based sandwiches and replace with anti-inflammatory alternatives such as sourdough or rye bread with smoked salmon or avocado fillings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham, scrambled egg and cheese croissant is a Western-style breakfast and lunch sandwich consisting of a buttery, flaky croissant pastry filled with sliced picnic ham, soft scrambled egg, and a slice of reduced-fat cheese. The croissant is made from butter-laminated yeast dough, producing a light, layered texture with a rich, slightly sweet flavour. The filling adds savoury, protein-rich elements. This item is widely sold at Singapore cafes, food courts, school canteens, and fast-food outlets, making it a common breakfast or working-lunch option. In Singapore it is also known as 火腿炒蛋芝士牛角包 (Chinese). Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 343 kcal/100g, with high total fat (24.92 g/100g, estimated saturated fat ~11.5 g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (20.98 g/100g, GI ~67), and moderate protein (10.29 g/100g). Sodium is elevated at 445 mg/100g, and dietary fibre is very low at 0.71 g/100g. Calcium is notably high at 241 mg/100g, contributed by the cheese component. A standard 180 g serving delivers approximately 617 kcal, 800 mg sodium, and 37.8 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of approximately 25. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, sodium, refined carbohydrate, and pro-inflammatory fat profile.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F08020040) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. Saturated fat is an adapted estimate consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference values for analogous croissant sandwich components and is documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain croissant, which is the dominant carbohydrate source. Local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as translational equivalents appropriate for Singapore food database use.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item (F08020040) as of 2026; no supplementary approved source provides these values for this specific composite item. saturated_fat_g (11.5 g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central reference values for butter-laminated croissant dough and reduced-fat cheese components, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this composite item. No zero-value core macros are present in this record. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on plain croissant from the University of Sydney GI Database; the protein and fat matrix of the sandwich may modestly lower the effective GI in practice. Potassium (22 mg/100g) and calcium (241 mg/100g) values are sourced directly from HPB. The halal status is listed as false as picnic ham is a pork-derived product and this item is not certified halal. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Local-language names added for Chinese (火腿炒蛋芝士牛角包), Malay (Croissant ham, telur hancur dan keju), Tamil (ஹாம், வேகவைத்த முட்டை மற்றும் சீஸ் க்ரோசாண்ட்), and Hindi (हैम, स्क्रैम्बल्ड एग और चीज़ क्रोइसां) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore food database records. All local-language strings appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed G5 — corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Fixed G6 — added mandatory local-language names for Chinese (火腿炒蛋芝士牛角包), Malay (Croissant ham, telur hancur dan keju), Tamil (ஹாம், வேகவைத்த முட்டை மற்றும் சீஸ் க்ரோசாண்ட்), and Hindi (हैम, स्क्रैम्बल्ड एग और चीज़ क्रोइसां) to local_names section.
Iteration 3: Fixed Q1 — local-language identity now complete with all four required Singapore languages populated.
Iteration 3: Fixed Q8 — added all four local-language strings to both aliases_by_language and flat aliases array, ensuring verbatim consistency. Aliases array expanded from 7 to 11 entries.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document the addition of local-language names and the ontology match_type correction.
